author: @aaron8526
text:
Fantastic stuff! I'm a EE student with an interest in audio; when I looked at electrostatic design, my mind naturally went to graphene (new materials to update old tech, etc).
I'm glad to see you're on top of that.
Any chance you could hook up an oscilloscope and show us source vs. speaker? Maybe give us some idea of what kind of impedance we're looking at, and what kind of frequency response? I'm curious what this would sound like if it were hooked to an amp circuit with appropriate impedance matching and filtering.

text:
Hi robert,

Is this a departure from classical electrostatic speaker design? I see the two aluminum electrodes and then the graphene in the middle, is that the asymmetric capacitor? or is it a a single plate with current flowing through the graphene which then vibrates the membrane? 

cheers,

replies.text: it's a different design - the current runs through the graphene and vibrates the membrane by setting up an equal an opposite charge

replies.author: @Yotakari14
replies.text: For electrostatics to reproduce bass the panel ideally needs to be the same size as the physical wavelength, for example to reproduce 20Hz (lowest end of human hearing range) you would need a panel at least 17 meters high (since 340m/s divided by 20Hz = 17m). I am unsure as to the width (currently studying electrostatics for my final project at university).

replies.author: @Yotakari14
replies.text: Yotakari14 This is why most modern ESLs use a hybrid design (ESL for mid and high frequencies and a standard cone woofer for bass extension)

replies.author: @oxiigen
replies.text:
Yotakari14
thank you

replies.author: @kcajmortsnnew1488
replies.text: actually, cut-off is determined by wavelength and displacement ,and IF you have a diaphragm capable of enough excursion ,it can be mounted in a large panel (think folding screen ,or in wall between two rooms)which prevent cancellation.....also,a single sided ESL only produced 1/2 wavefront....all this was done early on ,and by the 50's ,the golden age of sound reproduction ,was as highly developed as it got ,i suspect.....we moved on ,and now people seem satisfied to listen to plastic music out of plastic boxes....................16 Hz. will make you crap your pants ,by the way ,but almost no music below 32.....I'm into horns ,myself ,nearly as quick and thus clear ,as ESLs , but w/o the power demands...thus one can have natural sounding amps (tubes @ 5 Watts sufficient))..bt my big bass horn feeds up the wall into the attic,follows the roof line to the peak ,thus huge mouth...hopefully sub 39 notes...not tested yet.......ESLs ,as you know ,are capacitors,discovered in the 30's i believe from hearing caps "sing"..i never went to university ,just read it....

You need to use one of your single layer graphene sheets as the moveable diaphragm.  As I recall, the lighter the diaphragm the more efficient the speaker.
Maybe an ultra high end speaker company would buy some of your material?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: maybe lol - cheers mate

replies.author: @PietSpaans
replies.text:
The weight of the diaphragm is not that important, because the diafragm drags some air with it. I  think graphite (!!!) coated Mylar was already fine for making electrostatic speakers. There are more critical things that pose challenges for electrostatic speakers. 

I think it might be possible to squeeze the graphene between to mylar sheets, that way a very thin membrane is isolated from the stators, so higher voltage can be used to as  bias voltage and the elektrostatic speaker will be louder and greater excursion of the membrane will be allowed compared to graphite coated mylar which will spark at some distance from the stator, because there is nothing but air between the stator and the conductor (graphite).

The concept of building electrostatic speakers is very well explained by Roger Sanders in his Electrostatic Speaker Design Cookbook (accesible from the web).

Squeezing the Graphene between stretched mylar will be the hardest thing maybe, if it could be isolated my a more elegant means it would be better, also I wonder if the graphene can withstand the forces and if it can will it stretch enough to perform the excursions the music signal demands?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Hiya mate, thanks for that. I am now a bit stuck on what to say because the working principle behind this speaker and that behind the biased electrostatic speaker is very different. I am pretty sure from your general comments that you understand the concepts behind a biased electrostatic speaker very thoroughly so I will confine myself to the differences. In this speaker the base plate of the speaker was the only recipient of charge and the diaphragm had no graphene on it at all. well an induced charge exists or it wouldn't move. the graphene was used in the demo only to demonstrate - you could use anything at all as long as it conducted - so an aluminium foil would work well - probably better - as would a block of ali. The important thing is that it is stable and can carry a high voltage - 2-6 kV. The diaphragm is placed in front of the block and has no electrical connection to anything. The induced voltage is what causes the deflection and hence sound reproduction. My guess is that better reproduction would be from higher voltage, wider seperation of block and diapragm and better diaphragm material and design - i suspect a cell arrangement would be good.

Rob.

replies.author: @PietSpaans
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith The wonderful reproduction of electrostatic speakers has to do with the push pull mechanism. If the diafragm moves to one stator the coulom force on the other stator changes accordingly. With a high bias voltage the situation is symmetrical and the waveform of the sound will be the same as the waveform of the driving voltage. The push pull mechanism is sometimes also applied to woofers, that are screwed together on the front an where one gets a reversed phase signal. That way the asymmetry of the one woofer design is eliminated.
I looked a littel further in graphene and it seems that large sheets are still hard to produce. I guess it will be nice to start building a condenser mic first and then maybe an electret headphone. That could make electorstatic headphones obsolete maybe if it is possible to create a high permanent bias voltage. I think there is enough literature on the web, check for Stax headphones. I would recommend Roger Sanders book, it contains a lot of knowledge on engineering and about sound perception and it is just a google search away.
http://www.introni.it/pdf/Sanders%20-%20Electrostatic%20Loudspeaker%20Design%20Cookbook%201995.pdf

text: I do like the ideas. Teflon would work as it is an insulator. It sounds to me as if you are thinking of some kind of fluid based induction machine a bit like a bonetti machine but with a rotating fluid as opposed to a rotating disk. It's a really nice idea if you put you the fluid in a plastic pipe with a short metal section as a draw off point it could be pretty cool. Anyway, keep up the good work and let me know how you get on - Rob

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I'm not sure what kind of answer to give here because graphene shows some pretty weird effect in magnetic fields. For example because of its high mobility and minimum conductivity, and because of certain peculiarities explained in Pseudo-relativistic theory, graphene shows particularly interesting behavior just in the presence of a magnetic field and just with respect to the conductivity-quantization. Fascinating stuff

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I like the panacea boys. But it doesn't matter. The idea is now in the public domain having been disclosed to the world prior to any patent application and therefore now un patentable by anyone. Which means now everyone owns it.

author: @IndianaBoys
text:
In a first-to-file system, the right to the grant of a patent for a given invention lies with the first person to file a patent application for protection of that invention, regardless of the date of actual invention.

en.wikipedia (dot) org/wiki/First_to_file_and_fir­st_to_invent

Ashweth at Panacea may have some resources to keep your developments protected as Open SOurce:

panacea-bocaf (dot) org/about.htm

author: @IndianaBoys
text:
Robert, patent law just changed here in the US.  
First to file and first to invent are legal concepts that define who has the right to the grant of a patent for an invention. The first-to-file system is used in all countries[citation needed] including the United States, which switched to a first-to-file system on March 16, 2013 after the enactment of the America Invents Act.

en.wikipedia (dot) org/wiki/First_to_file_and_first_to_invent
